calendar.matters:
Mtn And Ntc Of Mtn On Petition To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar Oct-21-2010 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PETITIONER COALITION OF UNIVERSITY EMPLOYEES, INTERNATIONAL BROTHERHOOD OF TEAMSTERS PETITION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION. Appearances: Susan K. Garea of Beeson Tayer & Bodine (510.625.9700) for petitioner and moving party. No other appearances. The court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: granted. The court has considered the supplemental briefing by both parties. The 1/20/10 letter from the UC to CUE constitutes a contract to arbitrate. Standing order 100.4 (d) states that the president and those of his staff to whom he may delegate such authority are authorized to act as agents of the regents. Nothing in the standing order requires the delegation of authority to be in writing. Jones-Lindsay's job description in her declaration describes her duties, and she was acting within the scope of her employment and fulfilling the duties assigned to her as an agent of the office of the president and had the authority to bind the UC. The letter, followed by the actions of the UC in conformity with an intent to arbitrate provided mutual assent. Consideration is shown by petitioner's implied promise to forebear filing a PERB charge in exchange for the agreement to arbitrate. Promissory estoppel provides another ground for compelling arbitration. Estoppel does apply against government and public entities "where justice and right require it." Driscoll v. City of Los Angeles (1967) 67 Cal.2nd 297, 306. Promissory estoppel may not be invoked against a government entity if it would operate to defeat the effective operation of a policy adopted to protect the public. However, here, no public policy is threatened by applying the doctrine of promissory estoppel. Rather, a public policy is being upheld: the strong public policy in favor of arbitration. Order signed in open court. (302/CWW) Judge: Charlotte Walter Woolard, Reporter: Kent Gubbine CSR #5797
